focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 12

Eli started his shift before the light went, because Tuesday check-ins always stacked at the worst hour and the boiler room always fell behind by eight if he came in on time. The motel sat a little above the highway, close enough that truck brakes hissed through the cottonwoods every few minutes, far enough that the sound got soft at the edges. The laundry door stuck on damp days. He had to lean his shoulder into it, and the crack in his left rib answered back, a bright, mean line that made him hold still with one hand on the jamb until the pain settled into the usual ache. The rubber band on his wrist still held Marisol's sticky note from the midnight desk run, and the ink on `Fri 3 p.m. ET - June 640` had blurred where steam kept finding it.

Inside, the room held the same layered smell it always held: bleach, old carpet, damp terry cloth, and that mineral-sulfur heat from the springs that never washed all the way out of anything. He fed sheets into the folding machine, felt steam rise against his forearms, and tried to keep his breathing even while the roller took the corners straight. The silver scar at his hairline pulled when he frowned. He made himself stop touching it. He had been catching himself checking the scar in window glass all week, as if the face might change if he watched it enough.

Marisol came in with two clipboards under one arm and a pencil tucked through the loose knot of her braid. She had changed into the red zip hoodie she wore when the temperature dropped, sleeves shoved to her elbows so the pale burn mark on her right forearm showed every time she flipped a page. She always wrote in tiny block letters that looked like labels on specimen jars, and she licked the pad of her thumb before she turned each sheet, every time, without...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...Put your number as emergency contact with motel manager. Sorry for that surprise.

He sent that too.

He opened June's thread and typed:

June, I'm in Glenwood Springs working nights at a motel. I know I don't get to drop into your phone like nothing happened. I'm sending money Friday. You don't have to answer.

His thumb hovered over SEND until the cheap plastic keycap warmed under it. A stitch ripped along his left side when he leaned in, and he realized he was holding his breath hard enough to shake.

He deleted the message line by line. If he sent this before any transfer receipt existed, it would read like another midnight request for absolution dressed up as logistics. She had already paid that bill.

He set two alarms, one for medication and one for 8:45 p.m. The bathroom fan screamed when he turned it on. He kicked the housing twice, and it stopped, exactly as promised.

He stood at the sink and washed the coffee grounds from his hands. A black crescent of grime came out from under his thumbnail and spiraled down the drain. The mirror gave him back a face he recognized in pieces: the old forehead under the new seam, the mouth that could still make rules sound reasonable, the eyes that had spent weeks looking past exits.

When he lay down, the rib woke again in small deliberate stabs that matched his heartbeat. He put a folded towel under his left side to keep from rolling and watched morning light crawl across the cracked bathroom tile. Outside, a guest dragged a hard-shell suitcase over concrete, the wheels rattling like bones in a cup.

He had a key, a shift, a price, and a woman at the desk who would throw him out if he tried to convert pity into credit.

It was not redemption. It was a routine.

For now, routine was the only kind of shelter he could afford.
